kept.title: AI Engineer
kept.snippet: ## Job Description About the role The AI Engineer is the build engine of our AI practice. You turn business requirements into working AI solutions, fast, using coding agents and frontier models, then take the ones that prove their worth into production with the security, compliance, and reliability that enterprise clients demand. This is not a model-training role. We are not building or fine-tuning models from scratch. You build agentic workflows and LLM-powered applications on top of frontier models, wire them into real enterprise systems, and make them safe to run. [...] You will work as part of the delivery team alongside consultants, business analysts, and architects. The consultant carries the client relationship and the architect owns the hard architectural calls. You own the build and you make real engineering decisions, not just execute someone else’s spec. You are proactive about spotting better ways to build, and trusted to deliver without close supervision. What you will do Use coding agents to prototype AI solutions quickly against real client requirements, so the team can validate value before committing to a full build Build, deploy, and maintain LLM-powered applications and agents that align with enterprise architecture principles and standards, with evaluation, monitoring, and token tracking in place from day one [...] Business-level English, able to understand business requirements directly from clients and communicate clearly in calls and in writing Nice to have Familiar with Microsoft Azure, particularly Azure AI infrastructure, as our primary cloud for production AI Hands-on with Anthropic’s managed agent infrastructure (Claude managed agents, the agents API) Prior work on evaluation methods for LLM outputs Experience in e-commerce, or from an AI, agentic, or workflow automation background Comfortable presenting technical work and learnings to a non-engineering audience ## Additional Information How we will work together

Founded in 2016 and headquartered in Atlanta, Banyan operates more than 100 portfolio companies across North America, the UK, EU, and APAC. # Head of AI Engineering [...] Deep Software Breadth: 8+ years of hands-on software engineering with a proven track record of modernizing complex architectures and establishing cloud-native/DevSecOps practices. Production AI Experience: Documented history of shipping functional AI/ML systems end-to-end to real users (data pipelines, modeling, deployment, and monitoring). You treat AI tooling as the core foundation of engineering productivity. The "Build-Solo" Appetite: Experience as a founding or first AI engineer who is completely comfortable delivering baseline architecture independently before relying on a large team.

kept.title: Senior AI Agentic Developer - HR Ways - Hiring Tech Talent - London Area, United Kingdom | IT Jobs Watch
kept.snippet: What We Are Looking For Core Engineering Foundation: 6+ years of experience in enterprise backend engineering, microservices architecture, and cloud infrastructure (Docker, Kubernetes, CI/CD pipelines). Agentic AI Expertise: 2+ years of hands-on experience building and deploying production-level LLM applications, with a strong focus on state machines, multi-agent frameworks, and advanced RAG. Security & Governance Mindset: A strong understanding of least-privilege tool access, sandboxed execution, and full audit traceability within data-sensitive environments. Tooling Proficiency: Deep familiarity with Python or TypeScript, modern API Gateways, and AI observability tools. Experience: 6 + Years Rate: 500 GBP / day Location: London Sector: Banking [...] Agent Orchestration & Architecture: Design and deploy production-grade multi-agent workflows using advanced frameworks (e.g., LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen). Implement complex orchestration patterns including intent detection, dynamic routing, state management, and hierarchical topologies. Tool & Protocol Integration: Build and govern Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers to expose enterprise tools, internal systems, APIs, and data sources as structured, auditable agent tool surfaces. (Knowledge of A2A is highly advantageous). AI Gateways & Governance: Configure and scale AI Gateway platforms (LiteLLM, AWS Bedrock, Azure API Management, or Apigee AI Extensions) for multi-model routing, cost optimization, quota enforcement, and automated model fallback. [...] Evaluation & Observability: Establish production LLM evaluation frameworks (golden test sets, regression suites, LLM-as-a-judge, online A/B testing) and implement red-teaming for prompt injection and adversarial inputs. Track and optimize system performance via enterprise observability platforms (e.g., Langfuse). CI/CD & DevOps: Support and contribute to modern CI/CD pipelines tailored for agentic systems, utilizing containerized deployments, automated behavioral testing, feature flags, and progressive rollout strategies.

kept.title: feed.xml - Soft Skills Engineering Podcast
kept.snippet: Thanks for lending sanity to the abyss, —An increasingly arcane internal apps dev 2. I have been listening to your podcast regularly and am inspired by how the podcast and the community have grown. I am a developer with over 10 years of experience and have moved to Sweden from a country outside Europe, with the ambition to build my long-term life and career here. For several years, I have tried to take that step myself, but often encounter the same obstacle: I am told I need experience as an engineering manager — but without the role, I can’t get the experience, and without the experience, I can’t get the role. I have invested a lot of time and energy in developing myself: learning about leadership, coaching, communication, and team dynamics. [...] In this episode, Dave and Jamison answer these questions: 1. Hi Djavison, I’ve heard y’all say something along the lines of “this is the most exciting time to build software” in a few recent episodes. I’m glad that has been your experience and seems to be the experience of many others. But for me as someone 5 years into the career who is, thankfully, employed—I can’t help mourning a job that no longer exists. Obviously, there are still lots of us with the job title “software engineer” that create software. But, what I originally fell in love with doesn’t really seem like a thing you can get paid to do anymore. I now spend most of my development time reviewing code and making sure things work. Which feels way less rewarding and way more soul-sucking. [...] My job is mostly okay, but could be better because of the people in it. I joined a greenfield project a few years ago as my first software engineer role after transitioning from other data work. I grew up with the project and improved my engineering skills. A year ago we hired two new people. They had relevant experience and seemed to know what they were talking about in the interview, and had five & ten years of experience (aka, more than me).

Roles are tracked daily and typically appear within hours of being posted on company career pages. This gives early applicants an advantage before listings get flooded. ## Companies we track [...] • Focus on companies known to pay strong salaries • Only roles that are still active on company career pages We track engineering roles directly from company websites and surface opportunities at companies where senior engineers typically earn €100k+ depending on role and location. Why charge developers instead of companies? Most job boards get paid by companies. That creates an incentive to maximize listings, not quality. We charge developers, so we have zero reason to inflate job counts, promote sponsored listings, or prioritize volume over relevance. If a role isn't worth your time, it doesn't belong here. How do you decide which companies to include? We focus on companies known to pay strong compensation for senior engineers, typically €100k+ depending on role and location.

kept.snippet: # GST Registration for a Private Limited Company — Rule 14A Fast-Track vs Normal Registration: Which Should You Choose? No incorporation portal asks you this question at the time of GST registration — yet the answer determines whether your registration takes 3 days or up to 30, and whether you will need to file Form GST REG-32 later. Yet this single selection determines whether your GST registration is granted in **3 working days** or takes the standard **7 to 30 days**, whether your application is Aadhaar-authenticated or subject to physical site verification, and — for companies whose monthly B2B billing grows beyond a certain point — whether you will later need to file **Form GST REG-32** to exit a scheme you may not have realised you opted into. This article explains Rule 14A registration, normal registration, who should choose which, why the choice matters more for a Private Limited Company than it might first appear, and what Form GST REG-32 is for.
